content:
# Salt

The jar had been sitting on the counter for three days before she noticed the crust forming around the lid — white and granular, like something the jar was slowly exhaling.

She picked it up. The glass was cool on one side where it faced the window, warmer on the other. She turned it in her hands the way you turn a stone you've found on a path, not sure yet whether you'll keep it.

Her mother had packed it. That was the thing. Tucked into the cardboard box between the dish towels and the spare set of keys — a jar of coarse sea salt, the kind with the gray tint that means it came from somewhere specific, somewhere with tides.

She unscrewed the lid. The smell was faint and mineral, like the inside of a shell held to the ear. She pressed her thumb into the surface and lifted a few grains, then touched them to her tongue.

It tasted like the back of a hand after a long walk. It tasted like the particular August they'd spent at the coast, the summer her mother still wore her hair long, before the surgery, before the careful new vocabulary they'd both had to learn.

She put the lid back on. Set the jar in the cabinet, behind the pepper, where she'd see it every morning when she reached for the coffee.

That seemed right. That seemed like enough.

# Salt

The jar had been sitting on the counter for three days before she noticed the crust forming around the lid — white and granular, like something the jar was slowly exhaling.

She picked it up. The glass was cool on one side where it faced the window, warmer on the other. She turned it in her hands the way you turn a stone you've found on a path, not sure yet whether you'll keep it.

Her mother had packed it. That was the thing. Tucked into the cardboard box between the dish towels and the spare set of keys — a jar of coarse sea salt, the kind with the gray tint that means it came from somewhere specific, somewhere with tides.

She unscrewed the lid. The smell was faint and mineral, like the inside of a shell held to the ear. She pressed her thumb into the surface and lifted a few grains, then touched them to her tongue.

It tasted like the back of a hand after a long walk. It tasted like the particular August they'd spent at the coast, the summer her mother still wore her hair long, before the surgery, before the careful new vocabulary they'd both had to learn.

She put the lid back on. Set the jar in the cabinet, behind the pepper, where she'd see it every morning when she reached for the coffee.
